result: I spent last night watching videos of deep-sea creatures caught on camera: a glass squid glimmering like blown sugar, a dumbo octopus flapping its ear-fins like a cartoon elephant. They don’t know the world above them exists, and most of us will never enter theirs. Yet here we are, strangers sharing a planet, separated by a few miles of water and an eternity of language. I wonder if the squid, glowing in pulses, is speaking in Morse code nobody has learned to read. Meanwhile, humans bark opinions at each other across glowing rectangles, pretending certainty. Certainty feels like a warm coat, but it’s often made of someone else’s wool. I keep thinking about how the octopus has three hearts: when it swims, one of them stops; the animal literally chooses intimacy over motion. I, too, hold still when something tender brushes my life—an old song, a smell of cedar, the way my grandmother used to say my name as if it were spelled differently. Years after her death I still hear that version, syllables floating like plankton in the dark, feeding everything below. Maybe that’s why I keep returning to the ocean in my mind: it mirrors the unconscious, dark, pressurized, glowing with its own forgotten constellations. We are all bioluminescent when no one is watching, manufacturing private constellations to navigate by. I want to live as the deep-sea live: unashamed of gelatin, transparent when necessary, beautiful without bones.

I wonder if the squid, glowing in pulses, is speaking in Morse code nobody has learned to read. Meanwhile, humans bark opinions at each other across glowing rectangles, pretending certainty. Certainty feels like a warm coat, but it’s often made of someone else’s wool. I, too, hold still when something tender brushes my life—an old song, a smell of cedar, the way my grandmother used to say my name as if it were spelled differently. Years after her death I still hear that version, syllables floating like plankton in the dark, feeding everything below. Maybe that’s why I keep returning to the ocean in my mind: it mirrors the unconscious, dark, pressurized, glowing with its own forgotten constellations. We are all bioluminescent when no one is watching, manufacturing private constellations to navigate by. I want to live as the deep-sea live: unashamed of gelatin, transparent when necessary, beautiful without bones.
